figures.caption: Figure 1: Example configurations of two clients with slightly different objective vectors and the impact on the downstream decision vectors.

Figure 6: FG Ratios for both the polyhedral and strongly convex synthetic experiments. FG Ratio = 1 is marked with a red line, with ∼ 2.5% of clients being below the threshold. In the x-axis, the three-digit config code corresponds to the specific experiment. The first digit signifies whether there is (1) or is no (0) data imbalance. The second digit corresponds to objective heterogeneity (0-3) and the third constraint heterogeneity.

Figure 2: Boxplots of the relative regret for the two synthetic experimental settings. The orange boxplots correspond to local model performance, and the blue boxplots to federated model performance. Above each pair of boxplots is the fraction of clients with a lower regret under federated learning. Under no data imbalance, each client has the same sample size. Under data imbalance, half the clients have 10 times more samples than the rest of the federation. Each point in the boxplots corresponds to one client, averaged across 5 seeds.
Figure 9: Local versus global regret per client in the homogeneous data paradigm for the entropy portfolio problem.
Figure 8: Local versus global regret per client in the heterogeneous data paradigm for the fractional knapsack problem for data-rich (blue) versus data-scarce clients (orange).
Figure 10: Local versus global regret per client in the heterogeneous data paradigm for the entropy portfolio problem for data-rich (blue) versus data-scarce clients (orange).

Figure 4: Visualization of the data distribution of the energy pricing, and the volatility of the pricing among clients. The size of the nodes corresponds to the hours of energy production the client must satisfy, and the color corresponds to the 95th quantile of the client’s congestion pricing.
Figure 11: FG Difference versus Real Regret Difference for clients
Figure 5: The % improvement in downstream regret across the different agents in the PJM experiments.

Table 3: Detailed results of the PJM case study. DFLLOC: The downstream regret for each client when using SPO+ with a local-only model. DFLFED: The downstream regret for each client when using SPO+ with a federated learning model. 𝚫 𝑗 : The difference DFLLOC − DFLFED. 𝚫 𝑗%: The percent difference 𝚫 𝑗/DFLLOC. 𝑭𝑮Δ: The estimated FG difference, according to Remark 1 for each client.

Figure 3: Scatter plot of the computed FG Difference on the y-axis versus the empirical regret difference from the experiments. We plot one scatter plot for the knapsack experiment and one for the portfolio, with the ratio of agreement (percent of samples where both the FG Difference and the real difference share a sign) appearing in each subtitle. The top row corresponds to the balanced data regime, and the bottom row the imbalanced data.
Figure 7: Local versus global regret per client in the homogeneous data paradigm for the fractional knapsack problem.
Table 5: Experimental Results (mean ± 95% CI) for the constituent metrics of the Federated Gain Ratio across 4 experimental configurations.
Table 1: Notation used throughout the paper.
